meta.title: Kotlin Android
meta.description: Review checklist for Kotlin Android applications

items.text: Is UI state held in ViewModel, not in the Activity or Fragment?

baseContent.whatItMeans: ViewModel survives configuration changes (rotation, theme changes). Storing state in Activity/Fragment fields means it is lost on rotation.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Users rotate their devices, switch apps, and change themes. State in Activity fields is destroyed and recreated, causing data loss and visual glitches.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check Activities and Fragments for mutable state fields. These should be StateFlow or LiveData in a ViewModel.
baseContent.exampleComment: The list of items is stored in a Fragment field. On rotation, it will be empty. Move it to a ViewModel with StateFlow.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: ViewModel owns state. Activity/Fragment observes it.

items.text: Are coroutines launched in the correct scope (viewModelScope, lifecycleScope)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: viewModelScope cancels when the ViewModel is cleared. lifecycleScope cancels when the lifecycle is destroyed. GlobalScope never cancels and leaks.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Coroutines in GlobalScope or manually created scopes leak when the user navigates away. They waste resources and can update destroyed UI, causing crashes.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for GlobalScope.launch, CoroutineScope(Dispatchers...).launch. These should usually be viewModelScope.launch or lifecycleScope.launch.
baseContent.exampleComment: GlobalScope.launch here means this network call continues even after the user leaves the screen. Use viewModelScope.launch instead.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: viewModelScope for ViewModels. lifecycleScope for UI. Never GlobalScope.

items.text: Does the app handle process death and restoration correctly?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Android can kill your process when the app is backgrounded. On return, the system recreates Activities from saved state. ViewModel data is lost unless saved with SavedStateHandle.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Users expect to return to exactly where they were. Without SavedStateHandle or persistent storage, form data, scroll position, and navigation state are lost.
baseContent.howToVerify: Test with 'Don't keep activities' in developer options. Background the app and return. Is the state preserved?
baseContent.exampleComment: The draft form data in ViewModel is lost on process death. Use SavedStateHandle to preserve it across process recreation.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test with 'Don't keep activities'. Use SavedStateHandle for transient state.

items.text: Does back navigation behave correctly and predictably?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The back button should navigate to the previous screen or close the current overlay. With Navigation Component, the back stack should be managed declaratively.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Broken back navigation is the most common UX complaint on Android. Users expect the back button to undo the last navigation action.
baseContent.howToVerify: Test the back button from every screen. Check for popUpTo and launchSingleTop usage. Ensure deep links do not create duplicate back stack entries.
baseContent.exampleComment: Pressing back from the order confirmation takes the user to the splash screen instead of the order list. Check the nav graph's popUpTo settings.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test back navigation from every screen. Use popUpTo to manage the back stack.

items.text: Are memory leaks avoided (no Activity/Context in static fields)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Storing Activity or Context references in static fields, companion objects, or long-lived objects prevents garbage collection of the entire Activity.
baseContent.whyItMatters: An Activity leak retains its entire view hierarchy (often many MB). Multiple leaks can cause OutOfMemoryError.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for companion object properties holding Context, Activity, or View references. Check for anonymous inner classes that capture the outer Activity.
baseContent.exampleComment: This companion object holds a reference to the Activity. After rotation, the old Activity cannot be garbage collected. Use applicationContext or remove the reference.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never store Activity/Fragment in static fields. Use applicationContext for long-lived references.

items.text: Are unnecessary recompositions avoided?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Compose skips recomposition for composables whose parameters have not changed. Unstable parameters (mutable lists, lambdas created inline) prevent skipping.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Excessive recomposition causes jank and battery drain. A list item that recomposes on every frame makes scrolling laggy.
baseContent.howToVerify: Use the Layout Inspector's recomposition counts. Check for unstable parameters: List instead of ImmutableList, inline lambda creation, passing ViewModel directly.
baseContent.exampleComment: Passing viewModel directly to this composable makes it unstable. Extract the needed state as separate parameters.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Pass stable, minimal parameters to composables. Hoist state out.

items.text: Are remember and derivedStateOf used with correct keys?

baseContent.whatItMeans: remember caches a value across recompositions. If the inputs change, the key must change too, or the cached value becomes stale.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Missing keys cause stale data. Unnecessary keys cause excessive recomputation. derivedStateOf should be used for expensive computations that depend on state.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check remember calls. Do they include all inputs as keys? Check derivedStateOf for computations that should only rerun when specific state changes.
baseContent.exampleComment: remember { expensiveFilter(items) } does not include items as a key. When items changes, the filter result will be stale. Use remember(items) { ... }.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code: val filtered = remember { items.filter { it.active } } // stale when items changes

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Include all dependencies as remember keys. Use derivedStateOf for expensive derived state.

items.text: Are side effects managed with LaunchedEffect, not in composition?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Side effects (API calls, navigation, analytics) must not run during composition. Use LaunchedEffect, SideEffect, or DisposableEffect.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Composition can happen multiple times per frame. A side effect in composition will fire repeatedly, causing duplicate API calls, crashes, or infinite loops.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for API calls, navigation, or state mutations directly in @Composable function bodies without LaunchedEffect/SideEffect wrappers.
baseContent.exampleComment: This API call runs on every recomposition. Wrap it in LaunchedEffect(key) to run it only when the key changes.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never perform side effects during composition. Always use effect handlers.

items.text: Is state hoisted to the appropriate level?

baseContent.whatItMeans: State hoisting moves state up to the lowest common ancestor that needs it. Composables become stateless and reusable.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unhoisted state makes composables hard to test and reuse. It also makes it harder to persist state across configuration changes.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for mutableStateOf inside composables that should be controlled by a parent. Can the composable be made stateless with callbacks?
baseContent.exampleComment: The text field state is internal to this composable. Hoist it to the parent so the parent can validate and submit.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Stateless composables are reusable and testable. Hoist state up.

items.text: Are runtime permissions requested with rationale at the right time?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Dangerous permissions (camera, location, contacts) must be requested at runtime. Show a rationale before the system dialog, and handle denial gracefully.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Requesting permissions at launch without context causes users to deny them. Once denied twice, Android shows 'Don't ask again'. Requesting at the right moment with rationale increases grant rates.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that permission requests happen when the user initiates the feature (e.g., tapping 'Take Photo'), not at app startup. Verify denial is handled.
baseContent.exampleComment: Camera permission is requested on app launch. Move it to when the user taps the camera button, with a rationale dialog first.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Request permissions in context. Show rationale. Handle denial gracefully.

items.text: Does the app request only necessary permissions?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Only request permissions the app actually needs. Use photo picker instead of READ_EXTERNAL_STORAGE. Use fine location only when coarse is not sufficient.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Excessive permissions reduce trust, trigger Play Store warnings, and can cause rejection. Users uninstall apps that request too many permissions.
baseContent.howToVerify: Review AndroidManifest.xml permissions. For each, verify the app actually uses it. Check for alternatives that do not require permissions (photo picker, MediaStore).
baseContent.exampleComment: READ_EXTERNAL_STORAGE is declared but the app only picks photos. Use the Photo Picker API instead, which needs no permission.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Fewer permissions = more trust. Use permission-free alternatives when available.

items.text: Is user data handled according to privacy best practices?

baseContent.whatItMeans: PII should be encrypted at rest, not logged, and transmitted over HTTPS. Use EncryptedSharedPreferences for sensitive data. Clear data on logout.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Data breaches cause legal liability and user harm. Android's data safety section in Play Store requires accurate disclosure.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check what data is stored and how. Is PII in plain SharedPreferences? Is sensitive data in log statements? Is data cleared on sign-out?
baseContent.exampleComment: The auth token is stored in plain SharedPreferences. Use EncryptedSharedPreferences or the Jetpack Security library.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Encrypt PII at rest. Never log sensitive data. Clear on logout.

items.text: Is heavy work performed off the main thread?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Network calls, database queries, file I/O, and heavy computation must run on background dispatchers (Dispatchers.IO or Dispatchers.Default), not Dispatchers.Main.
baseContent.whyItMatters: The main thread renders UI at 60fps. Blocking it for more than 16ms causes dropped frames. Blocking for 5+ seconds triggers an ANR (Application Not Responding) dialog.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for I/O or computation in viewModelScope.launch without withContext(Dispatchers.IO). Enable StrictMode in debug builds to catch violations.
baseContent.exampleComment: This database query runs on the main thread. Wrap it in withContext(Dispatchers.IO) to avoid ANRs.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: I/O on Dispatchers.IO. Computation on Dispatchers.Default. UI updates on Main.

items.text: Are lists using LazyColumn/LazyRow instead of Column/Row with forEach?

baseContent.whatItMeans: LazyColumn only composes and renders visible items. A regular Column with forEach composes all items upfront, causing lag on large lists.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A Column with 1000 items composes all 1000 at once. LazyColumn only composes the ~15 visible items, providing smooth scrolling.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for Column { items.forEach { Item(it) } } patterns. These should be LazyColumn { items(items) { Item(it) } }.
baseContent.exampleComment: This Column renders all 500 orders at once. Use LazyColumn with items() for smooth scrolling.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: LazyColumn for scrollable lists. Column only for small, fixed-size groups.

items.text: Are images loaded and cached efficiently?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use Coil or Glide for image loading. They handle caching, resizing, memory management, and lifecycle awareness. Never load full-resolution bitmaps manually.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Loading a 4000x3000 photo into memory without resizing uses ~48MB. In a list, this quickly causes OutOfMemoryError.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for BitmapFactory.decodeStream or manual bitmap loading. These should use Coil's AsyncImage or Glide's composable integration.
baseContent.exampleComment: This manually loads the full bitmap into memory. Use Coil's AsyncImage composable, which handles caching and resizing automatically.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use Coil or Glide. Never decode full bitmaps manually.

items.text: Is app startup optimized to avoid blocking the first frame?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Heavy initialization (DI, analytics, databases) in Application.onCreate or the first Activity delays the first frame. Use lazy initialization or App Startup library.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Users perceive startup time directly. More than 500ms to first frame feels slow. Heavy initialization blocks the main thread.
baseContent.howToVerify: Profile startup with Android Studio's profiler. Check Application.onCreate for synchronous init that could be deferred.
baseContent.exampleComment: Database initialization in Application.onCreate adds 300ms to startup. Use lazy initialization so it runs when first accessed.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Defer heavy initialization. Only do what is needed for the first frame.

items.text: Are scope functions (let, run, with, apply, also) used appropriately?

baseContent.whatItMeans: let for nullable chains, apply for configuring objects, run for computing a result, also for side effects, with for calling multiple methods on an object.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Misusing scope functions makes code harder to read. Using let where apply is appropriate, or nesting multiple lets, creates confusing chains.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for deeply nested scope functions (more than 2 levels). Check that the chosen function matches the intent.
baseContent.exampleComment: This nested let/let/also chain is hard to follow. Extract the inner logic into a named function.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good
codeExamples.code:
val city = user?.address?.city ?: return
updateCity(user.id, city)

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Scope functions should simplify, not complicate. If nesting more than 2, refactor.

items.text: Are data classes used for value types and DTOs?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Data classes provide equals, hashCode, toString, copy, and destructuring automatically. Use them for types whose identity is defined by their data.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Manual equals/hashCode implementations are error-prone and tedious. Data classes eliminate this entire category of bugs.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new classes with only properties and no behavior. Are they data classes? Watch for mutable properties in data classes used as map keys.
baseContent.exampleComment: This UserDto class manually implements equals and hashCode. Convert to a data class to get them automatically.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use data class for DTOs and value objects. Watch out for mutable properties.

items.text: Are sealed classes/interfaces used for restricted type hierarchies?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Sealed classes restrict which types can extend them. Combined with when expressions, the compiler ensures all subtypes are handled.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Sealed hierarchies make impossible states unrepresentable. Adding a new subtype forces all when expressions to be updated.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for enum-like hierarchies that carry different data per variant. These are better as sealed classes. Check when expressions for else branches that should be exhaustive.
baseContent.exampleComment: This UiState uses three boolean flags. A sealed class with Loading, Success, Error subclasses makes invalid states impossible.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
data class UiState(
    val isLoading: Boolean,
    val data: List<Item>?,
    val error: String?
)

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Sealed classes eliminate impossible states. Use them for UI state, results, and events.

items.text: Is Kotlin's null safety used correctly without excessive !! operators?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The !! operator throws NullPointerException. Kotlin's type system is designed to make nulls explicit. Using !! defeats this safety.
baseContent.whyItMatters: !! converts a compile-time null check into a runtime crash. It is the Kotlin equivalent of 'trust me, this is never null' and is often wrong.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for !! in the diff. Each one is a potential NPE. Replace with ?., ?.let, ?:, or requireNotNull with a message.
baseContent.exampleComment: user!! will crash if the user is null. Use user ?: return for early exit, or requireNotNull(user) { "User must be set" } for a clear error.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every !! is a potential crash. Use safe calls and requireNotNull instead.

items.text: Does the app handle offline scenarios gracefully?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The app should show cached data when offline, queue writes for later, and clearly communicate connectivity status to the user.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Mobile networks are unreliable. Tunnels, elevators, and poor coverage are common. An app that crashes or shows empty screens offline feels broken.
baseContent.howToVerify: Enable airplane mode and navigate through the app. Can the user still see previously loaded data? Are write operations queued?
baseContent.exampleComment: The home screen shows a blank white page when offline. Show cached data with a subtle offline indicator instead.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Show cached data offline. Queue writes. Communicate status clearly.

items.text: Is the network layer configured with timeouts, retries, and error handling?

baseContent.whatItMeans: OkHttp/Retrofit should have connect, read, and write timeouts. Retries should use exponential backoff. Errors should be mapped to domain types.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without timeouts, a hung server blocks the coroutine forever. Without retries, transient failures surface to the user unnecessarily.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check OkHttpClient configuration. Are timeouts set? Is there a retry interceptor? Do API errors map to sealed result types?
baseContent.exampleComment: The OkHttpClient has no timeouts configured. Add .connectTimeout(10, SECONDS) and .readTimeout(30, SECONDS).
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always set timeouts. Add retries with backoff for transient failures.

items.text: Is certificate pinning configured for sensitive API calls?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Certificate pinning ensures the app only trusts specific certificates, preventing man-in-the-middle attacks even if a CA is compromised.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without pinning, any CA-signed certificate is trusted. A compromised CA or corporate proxy can intercept all traffic.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for CertificatePinner in OkHttp or network_security_config.xml. Ensure pins include a backup and have a rotation plan.
baseContent.exampleComment: Sensitive API calls have no certificate pinning. Add a CertificatePinner with the server's public key hash and a backup pin.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Pin certificates for sensitive APIs. Include backup pins for rotation.

items.text: Are ViewModels tested with fake repositories?

baseContent.whatItMeans: ViewModel tests should use fake implementations of repositories/use cases, not mocks. Fakes are easier to maintain and test behavior rather than implementation.
baseContent.whyItMatters: ViewModels contain the app's business logic. Without tests, regressions go unnoticed until QA or production.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for test classes corresponding to new ViewModels. Do they use Turbine for testing StateFlow emissions?
baseContent.exampleComment: This new ViewModel has no tests. Add tests with a FakeOrderRepository and Turbine to verify state emissions.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test ViewModels with fakes and Turbine. They are the most valuable tests on Android.

items.text: Are Compose UI tests using semantic matchers?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Compose tests find elements by semantics (content description, text, test tag) not by implementation details. Use composeTestRule.onNodeWithText/onNodeWithTag.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Tests that depend on implementation details break on every UI change. Semantic matchers test what the user sees.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check UI tests for hardcoded indices or internal composable names. These should use semantic matchers.
baseContent.exampleComment: This test finds elements by index. Use onNodeWithText("Submit") or onNodeWithContentDescription for stable selectors.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test what the user sees with semantic matchers, not internal composable structure.

items.text: Are coroutine tests using TestDispatcher and runTest?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Coroutine tests should use runTest with StandardTestDispatcher or UnconfinedTestDispatcher. This controls virtual time and makes tests deterministic.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Tests with real dispatchers are flaky due to timing. runTest skips delays and advances virtual time, making tests fast and deterministic.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for runBlocking in tests (should usually be runTest). Verify that ViewModels accept a CoroutineDispatcher parameter for testability.
baseContent.exampleComment: This test uses runBlocking with Thread.sleep. Use runTest with advanceTimeBy for deterministic timing.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use runTest, not runBlocking. Inject dispatchers for testability.

items.text: Do images and icons have content descriptions for screen readers?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every meaningful image and icon must have a contentDescription. Decorative images should use contentDescription = null to be skipped by TalkBack.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without content descriptions, blind users cannot understand what icons and images represent. TalkBack reads 'unlabeled' which is useless.
baseContent.howToVerify: Enable TalkBack and navigate the screen. Are all interactive elements announced meaningfully? Are decorative elements skipped?
baseContent.exampleComment: This Icon composable has no contentDescription. Add a descriptive string like 'Delete order' or null if purely decorative.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Meaningful images get descriptions. Decorative images get null.

items.text: Are touch targets at least 48dp?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Material Design recommends 48dp minimum touch target size. Compose's Modifier.minimumInteractiveComponentSize enforces this.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Small touch targets are frustrating for all users and inaccessible for users with motor impairments. The accessibility scanner flags targets under 48dp.
baseContent.howToVerify: Run the Accessibility Scanner on each screen. Check that clickable elements are at least 48dp in both dimensions.
baseContent.exampleComment: This icon button is 24dp. Add Modifier.minimumInteractiveComponentSize() or increase the padding to reach 48dp.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: 48dp minimum touch targets. Use minimumInteractiveComponentSize.
